title: Prologue & Episodes I–II: The Decree
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: ANTIGONE, daughter of Oedipus. ISMENE, her sister. CREON, King of Thebes. HAEMON, Creon's son, betrothed to Antigone. TEIRESIAS, the blind prophet. A SENTRY. A MESSENGER. EURYDICE, Creon's wife. CHORUS of Theban Elders.
PROLOGUE
(Before the palace. ANTIGONE and ISMENE speak privately.)

ANTIGONE: Ismene—my sister—of all the evils that Oedipus left us, do you know any that Zeus has not fulfilled upon us both? There is nothing—nothing bitter, nothing ruinous, no shame, no dishonor—that I have not seen in our calamities. And now this: Creon has issued a proclamation. Our brother Eteocles, who fell defending Thebes, shall have honorable burial. But Polynices, who came against the city with foreign arms—his body shall lie unburied, unwept, a feast for birds and dogs.
ISMENE: But—if Creon has decreed it—
ANTIGONE: He has. And I intend to bury Polynices. Will you help me?
ISMENE: (frightened) Antigone—you cannot. He has forbidden it. The penalty is death.
ANTIGONE: He is my brother. The law of the gods demands that the dead be given burial. What right has any human law to override that?
ISMENE: I don't say Creon is right. But I am afraid. We are only women—and alone—and powerless against men who govern. I beg you—do not do this.
ANTIGONE: Then I will do it alone. (She goes.)
EPISODE I
(CREON has proclaimed the decree publicly. A SENTRY reports that someone has scattered dust over Polynices' body—the ritual token of burial.)
CREON: Who dared this? Bring me the man—or woman—who defied my law!
SENTRY: I will try, sir. But—honestly—I don't know who it was. We found no footprints, no sign of a tool. Just the dust.
CREON: This is the work of men paid to subvert me. Go. Find the criminal. Bring him to me.
EPISODE II
(The SENTRY returns, leading ANTIGONE.)
SENTRY: Here is the one, my lord. We caught her in the very act—covering the body again, with her bare hands.
CREON: (turning to Antigone) Did you know of my proclamation?
ANTIGONE: Yes. It was public.
CREON: And still you dared to disobey?

ANTIGONE: Yes. Because your proclamation was not from Zeus, nor from Justice—the Justice that lives among the gods below. I did not think your edicts strong enough to override the unwritten and unfailing laws of heaven. Those laws are not of today or yesterday; they live forever, and no one knows their origin in time. I was not afraid of any mortal's punishment—afraid enough to answer to the gods for breaking their law.

CREON: (coldly) The stubborn spirit fails most often. The strongest iron shatters in the fire. She knew the law and broke it. And she boasts of it. If I allow a woman to defy me—what becomes of order? She must die.

title: Episodes III–V: The Consequences
paragraphs: EPISODE III
(HAEMON, Creon's son, enters. He is calm.)
CREON: You have heard, my son, what I have decided about Antigone? She is to die. What do you say?
HAEMON: Father—I am yours. I defer to your wisdom. No marriage could be worth more to me than your good counsel.
CREON: (pleased) Well said, my boy. This is how it should be—a son who respects his father's judgment.

HAEMON: But—I should also tell you what I hear in the city. The people—quietly, not openly—they say she does not deserve to die for an act of such simple piety. They say: to bury one's brother—is this not holy?
CREON: What the people say is not law.
HAEMON: Father, no man possesses all wisdom. Even the wisest man may learn. Those who refuse to hear others—they find, when they are opened up, that they are empty.
CREON: Are you preaching to me?
HAEMON: I say what is true. If you kill this woman who is to be my wife—you kill her for nothing. I beg you. Think again.
CREON: She shall die before her husband's eyes.
HAEMON: Then she will not die alone. You will never see me again.
(He goes.)
EPISODE IV
(ANTIGONE is brought out for her entombment—alive, walled up in a cave.)

ANTIGONE: O tomb, O marriage chamber, O everlasting prison, where I go to join my own—Persephone has received them all—and last of all, most miserably, I go too, before my life is done. And yet—I hope to come to my father, and to you, my mother, and to you, my brother Eteocles. But you, Polynices—it was for your burial that I won this reward. And I honored you rightly. I would not have done this for a husband, or for children—they could be replaced. But with both parents dead, there is no brother for me anymore. That was the law I honored. And Creon says I was wrong. I am being led away—a prisoner—because I honored the law of the gods.
CHORUS: Yet even now you might have hoped—
ANTIGONE: No. No one will weep for me. No friend, no marriage-hymn, no kindling of the torch. I go to the underground chambers of the dead. (She is led away.)
EPISODE V
(TEIRESIAS enters.)

TEIRESIAS: Creon—listen. I sit in my accustomed seat of augury, where all the birds come, and I heard them screeching, tearing one another—and I knew: the altars of the city are defiled by birds and dogs who have fed on the flesh of Polynices. The gods will not accept our prayers or offerings. You have done this. Reverse your decree. Give the dead burial.
CREON: Not for your art, old prophet, not for gold—

TEIRESIAS: Then hear this. Before the sun has run a course or two, you shall pay for it—the death of one from your own blood. The furies are waiting. You made this choice. Now live with the consequences.
(He goes. CREON stands shaken.)
CHORUS: Sire—he has never prophesied a false thing.
CREON: (after a long silence) I know. Go. Take men with you. Go to the cave. And bring the body of Polynices for burial. Go quickly.
(He goes out. But it is too late.)

title: Exodos: The Ruin of Creon
paragraphs: (A MESSENGER enters.)
MESSENGER: Citizens of Thebes—listen. Creon was once a man to be envied. He saved this city in its hour of need. He ruled with power and with purpose. And now—everything he had is gone.
CHORUS: What has happened?
MESSENGER: Haemon is dead. By his own hand.
(EURYDICE, Creon's wife, has come out to listen.)

MESSENGER: The king went to the cave where Antigone was entombed. The stone was moved aside and we heard—a sound—a voice—Haemon's voice. He had gone in before us. And inside—Antigone had hanged herself with her veil. And Haemon was holding her, weeping over her. When he saw his father he said nothing—he drew his sword and rushed at him. Creon leapt back. And then Haemon turned the blade on himself and died in her arms.
(EURYDICE goes back into the palace without speaking.)
CHORUS: This silence—what does it mean?
(CREON enters, bearing HAEMON's body.)
CREON: Look at me. Look on a foolish man, whose rigidity has killed his own son. I have learned this too late. The wisdom that I should have had, I did not have.
(A MESSENGER comes from the palace.)
MESSENGER: The queen—Eurydice—is dead. She heard everything. She cursed you for Haemon's death and for Megareus before him, and she stabbed herself.
(CREON falls.)
CREON: I am nothing. I have nothing. All is gone. Lead me away. I was mad—I see it now. The gods have crushed me under their hand.
CHORUS: (to the audience) Great words of wisdom are the surest blessings. The proud do not escape. Great blows make great wisdom in old age—but always too late.
CURTAIN.
